description: Golden dataset for RAGAS evaluation of the Incident RCA Assistant

questions.question: What causes a DB connection pool exhaustion in the auth-service?
questions.ground_truth: DB connection pool exhaustion in auth-service is caused by connection leaks where connections are not properly closed in error handling paths, particularly in the /validate-token endpoint. When errors occur, the connection is not returned to the pool, causing gradual pool exhaustion.

questions.question: How do I fix a connection pool exhaustion immediately?
questions.ground_truth: Immediate fixes include: 1) Kill idle connections older than 5 minutes using pg_terminate_backend, 2) Temporarily increase max_connections, 3) Restart affected application pods to reset connection state. Long-term: implement PgBouncer, add connection leak detection, set pool configuration with max_idle_time.

questions.question: What are the symptoms of a memory leak in a Java service?
questions.ground_truth: Symptoms include: steady heap/RSS growth over time, increasing GC frequency and pause duration, GC overhead consuming more than 25% CPU, and eventual OOMKill events. Key metrics to watch are heap usage >75% of limit, GC pauses >500ms, and RSS growth rate >10MB/hour.

questions.question: How do I roll back a Kubernetes deployment?
questions.ground_truth: Use kubectl rollout undo deployment/<service-name> -n production to rollback to the previous version. To rollback to a specific revision, add --to-revision=<N>. Verify with kubectl rollout status and kubectl get pods. Always check deployment history first with kubectl rollout history.

questions.question: How does a timeout in inventory-service cascade to the API gateway?
questions.ground_truth: Inventory-service slowdown causes order-service timeouts as it depends on inventory checks. Order-service exhausts retries and begins returning 504s. The api-gateway thread pool fills up waiting for order-service responses, and when it reaches capacity, the gateway becomes unresponsive, returning 504 Gateway Timeout for all requests.

questions.question: What is the recommended circuit breaker configuration?
questions.ground_truth: Recommended configuration: sliding window size of 10, failure rate threshold of 50%, wait duration in open state of 30 seconds, 3 permitted calls in half-open state, slow call rate threshold of 80%, and slow call duration threshold of 2 seconds. Implement using Resilience4j for Java or pybreaker for Python.

questions.question: What caused the payment service OOM kills in August 2024?
questions.ground_truth: The payment-service v3.1.0 had an unbounded in-memory TransactionCache that stored every processed transaction without TTL or max-size eviction. Under production load of ~200 transactions per minute, the cache grew linearly until the JVM heap was exhausted, triggering GC storms and OOMKill events.

questions.question: Summarize the cascading timeout incident from November 2024.
questions.ground_truth: A missing index on the sku column of the stock table caused full table scans in inventory-service during a flash sale. Query latency increased from 15ms to 2400ms+, causing cascading timeouts through order-service and api-gateway. The gateway thread pool was exhausted making it unresponsive. A DBA applied an emergency index which resolved the issue. 340 orders were affected over a 25-minute incident.

questions.question: What timeout tuning strategy should I follow for microservices?
questions.ground_truth: Follow the golden rule: downstream timeout must be shorter than upstream timeout. For example: API Gateway 30s > Order Service 10s > Inventory Service 3s > Database 1s. Connection timeout should be 1-3 seconds, read/response timeout 3-10 seconds depending on operation, and total request timeout should account for retries.

questions.question: What are common causes of memory leaks in production services?
questions.ground_truth: Common causes include: 1) Unclosed connections (DB, HTTP, file handles not closed in error paths), 2) Unbounded caches without TTL or max-size eviction, 3) Circular references preventing garbage collection, 4) Large object retention, 5) Event listener leaks (registering without deregistering), 6) Static collections that accumulate items without cleanup.
